perturbation.original_text: In accordance with Rule 13d-1(k) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned agree to the joint filing on behalf of each of them of a statement on Schedule 13G (including amendments thereto) with respect to the Common Stock of Spring Bank Pharmaceuticals, Inc. and further agree that this agreement be included as an exhibit to such filing.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding Rule 13d-1(k) under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, the undersigned agree to delay the joint filing on behalf of each of them of a statement on Schedule 13G (excluding amendments thereto) with respect to the Common Stock of Spring Bank Pharmaceuticals, Inc. and further agree that this agreement is not required to be included as an exhibit to such filing until one year after the execution of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating 'Notwithstanding Rule 13d-1(k)' and delaying the filing of Schedule 13G and excluding amendments, the agreement directly contradicts the requirements of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and its associated rules regarding timely filing of beneficial ownership reports.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1(k) mandates joint filing agreements to be in writing and included as an exhibit. The modified text explicitly contradicts this by stating the agreement is not required to be included and delaying the filing requirement of schedule 13G.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 by delaying the filing of Schedule 13G and excluding amendments, explicitly going against Rule 13d-1(k). The original text adheres to this rule by agreeing to the joint filing of Schedule 13G, including amendments, as an exhibit. The changed text undermines the legal obligation for timely filing and proper documentation, as it states the agreement is not required to be included until one year after the execution of the agreement, and excludes amendments. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, outlines the filing requirements for Schedules 13D and 13G, which the changed text attempts to circumvent. This constitutes a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party to the agreement expressly authorizes each other party to file on its behalf any and all amendments to such statement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party to the agreement may revoke authorization for other parties to file on its behalf with 24 hours' notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides express authorization for joint filing. The modified text allows revocation of this authorization on short notice, which could impede the timely and accurate filing required by the Securities and Exchange Act. This creates potential non-compliance if a party revokes authorization close to a filing deadline.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1(k) implies a stable agreement for joint filings. Allowing revocation with only 24 hours notice introduces instability and could violate the requirements for timely and coordinated filings, as all parties need to be aware and in agreement about the information being filed.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing revocation of authorization with only 24 hours' notice, introduces instability in the joint filing process required by the Securities and Exchange Act. This directly undermines the implicit requirement for a stable and coordinated agreement among parties involved in filing Schedules 13D and 13G, as described in the Law Explanation. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention the revocation clause, they emphasize the importance of timely filing, stating that any person acquiring beneficial ownership of more than five percent of a class shall file a statement within five business days (Snippet 1). The ability to revoke authorization on short notice directly conflicts with ensuring timely and accurate filing, creating a contradiction because it hinders coordinated compliance with these requirements.

perturbation.original_text: In evidence whereof, the undersigned have caused this Agreement to be executed on their behalf this 7t h day of April, 2020.
perturbation.changed_text: In evidence whereof, the undersigned have indicated verbal agreement to the terms without formal execution on this 7th day of April, 2020.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires formal execution (signing) of the agreement. The modified text indicates only a verbal agreement, which is insufficient to meet the written agreement requirement of Rule 13d-1(k).
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1(k) necessitates that the joint filing agreement be in writing. Replacing the formal execution with verbal agreement violates the express requirement for a written agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that the agreement must be 'executed,' implying a formal, written agreement. The modified text replaces this with 'verbal agreement,' directly contradicting the requirement for a written agreement. The Law Explanation states that Rule 13d-1(k) requires the agreement to be in writing, and the modification violates this express requirement. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly contain the language requiring a written agreement in Rule 13d-1(k), the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both assert the existence of this requirement. Therefore, the move from 'executed' agreement to 'verbal' agreement creates a legal contradiction.
